title: An object with mass 500 g moves along x-axis with speed v = 4√x m/s. The force a
subject: mechanics
scenario: An object with mass 500 g moves along x-axis with speed v = 4$\sqrt{x}$ m/s. The force acting on the object is?

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they try to find acceleration as dv/dt. But velocity is given as a function of x, not t. What should you do?

options.text: Use a = v(dv/dx) since v is a function of x
options.feedback: Exactly! When v = f(x), the chain rule gives a = v(dv/dx). This is the key move.

options.text: Differentiate v = 4$\sqrt{x}$ directly w.r.t. time to get a
options.feedback: You don't have v(t) — you have v(x). Differentiating w.r.t. time needs x(t), which isn't given. Use the chain rule: a = dv/dt = (dv/dx)(dx/dt) = v(dv/dx). This bypasses time entirely.

options.text: Use v² = u² + 2as to find a directly
options.feedback: v² = u² + 2as assumes constant acceleration — but you haven't proven that yet. You need to find acceleration first, not assume it. Derive it from v(x) using a = v(dv/dx).

steps.tip: v = f(x) — use a = v(dv/dx). v = f(t) — use a = dv/dt.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: $v = 4x^{1/2}$. What is dv/dx?

options.text: $2 / \sqrt{x}$
$4 / \sqrt{x}$
options.feedback: You forgot to multiply the coefficient. d/dx of 4x^(1/2) = 4 × ½ × $x^{-1/2}$. The 4 × ½ = 2, not 4. Result: 2/$\sqrt{x}$.

options.text: $2\sqrt{x}$
options.feedback: You brought the power down correctly (4 × ½ = 2) but didn't reduce the exponent. $x^{1/2}$ becomes $x^{-1/2}$ = 1/$\sqrt{x}$ after differentiation, not $\sqrt{x}$. Answer: 2/$\sqrt{x}$.

steps.tip: d/dx of x^n = n·$x^{n-1}$. Never rush the constant.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: Now compute a = v · (dv/dx) = 4$\sqrt{x}$ · (2/$\sqrt{x}$). What happens?

options.text: $\sqrt{x}$ cancels out — acceleration = 8 m/s² (constant!)
options.feedback: Beautiful cancellation. $\sqrt{x}$ ×(1/$\sqrt{x}$) = 1. So a = 8 m/s², constant and independent of x.

options.text: You get 8x, acceleration depends on position
options.feedback: Check your exponent algebra. $\sqrt{x}$ ×(1/$\sqrt{x}$) = $x^{1/2}$ ×$x^{-1/2}$ = x^0 = 1, not x. The x-dependence cancels completely. a = 4 ×2 = 8 m/s², a constant.

options.text: You get 8/x, acceleration decreases with x
options.feedback: You inverted incorrectly. $\sqrt{x}$ × (1/$\sqrt{x}$) = 1, not 1/x. The half-powers cancel: (+1/2) + (-1/2) = 0. So a = 8 m/s² — constant and independent of position.

steps.tip: When terms cancel to give constant acceleration, expect a clean final answer.

steps.label: LOCK THE ANSWER
steps.prompt: $F = ma$. Mass is 500 g and a = 8 m/s². What is the force?

options.text: 4 N (convert 500 g = 0.5 kg first)
options.feedback: Nailed it. 0.5 kg ×8 m/s² = 4 N. Unit conversion is non-negotiable.

options.text: 4000 N (used 500 directly without converting)
options.feedback: You plugged in 500 directly without converting. F = ma needs kg, not grams. 500 g = 0.5 kg. So F = 0.5 ×8 = 4 N, not 500 ×8 = 4000 N. Always convert to SI first.

options.text: 8 N (forgot to multiply by mass)
options.feedback: That's just the acceleration value, not the force. F = ma, not F = a. Multiply by mass: 0.5 kg × 8 m/s² = 4 N. Don't skip the mass.

steps.tip: Always convert to SI units before plugging into any formula.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: We got F = 4 N (constant). Does this make physical sense for v = 4$\sqrt{x}$?

options.text: Yes — constant force means constant acceleration, consistent with v — $\sqrt{x}$
options.feedback: Exactly. From v² = 16x, compare with v² = u² + 2as — 2a = 16, a = 8 = constant. Constant a means constant F. Everything checks out.

options.text: No — force should increase because speed increases with x
options.feedback: Speed increasing doesn't mean force is increasing — it means a force exists. v² = 16x has the form v² = 2as, confirming constant acceleration — constant force. Increasing v is the effect, not the cause.

options.text: No — force should be zero since there's no explicit time dependence
options.feedback: Force doesn't require explicit time dependence. The velocity changes with position, meaning the object accelerates, meaning a force acts. The chain rule a = v(dv/dx) finds it without any time variable.

steps.tip: Compare your result's form with known kinematic equations as a quick check.

title: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio 3:4
subject: mechanics
scenario: If the radius of curvature of the path of two particles of same mass are in the ratio 3:4, then in order to have constant centripetal force, their velocities will be in the ratio of?

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: Two particles, same mass, same centripetal force, different radii. Which formula connects F, m, v, and r?

options.feedback: Right. F = mv²/r is the go-to when velocity (not angular velocity) is what you're solving for.

options.text: $F = m\omega^{2}r$
options.feedback: F = mω²r uses angular velocity ω, not linear velocity v. You'd need an extra conversion step (v = ωr). Since the problem asks for velocity ratio directly, F = mv²/r is cleaner.

options.text: $F = mv/r$
options.feedback: F = mv/r is dimensionally wrong. Force has units of kg·m/s², but mv/r gives kg·m/s / m = kg/s — that's not force. The correct formula has v², not v: F = mv²/r.

steps.tip: Solving for v? Use F = mv²/r. Solving for ω? Use F = mω²r.

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they assume v — r when F and m are constant. From F = mv²/r with F, m constant, what's the real relation?

options.text: v² — r, so v — $\sqrt{r}$
options.feedback: Spot on. Rearrange: v² = Fr/m. With F and m constant, v² — r, meaning v — $\sqrt{r}$.

options.text: v — r (direct proportionality)
options.feedback: Don't just eyeball the formula. Rearrange: v² = Fr/m. It's v² that's proportional to r, not v. Taking the square root: v — $\sqrt{r}$. Missing the root changes your entire ratio.

options.text: v — 1/r (inverse proportionality)
options.feedback: v isn't inversely proportional to r. From F = mv²/r — v² = Fr/m. With F and m constant, v² increases with r. So v — $\sqrt{r}$ — bigger radius needs more speed, not less.

steps.tip: Always isolate the variable you need BEFORE writing proportionalities.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: v — $\sqrt{r}$ and r₁:r₂ = 3:4. What is v₁:v₂?

options.text: $\sqrt{3} : \sqrt{4} = \sqrt{3} : 2$
options.feedback: Clean finish. $\sqrt{3}$ : $\sqrt{4}$ simplifies to $\sqrt{3}$ : 2. That's your answer.

options.text: 3 : 4 (forgot the square root)
options.feedback: You forgot to apply the square root. v — $\sqrt{r}$, so the ratio of velocities = $\sqrt{r}$atio of radii = $\sqrt{3}$ : $\sqrt{4}$ = $\sqrt{3}$ : 2. Not 3:4.

options.text: 4 : 3 (inverted the ratio)
options.feedback: You flipped the ratio. v₁ corresponds to r₁ = 3, v₂ to r₂ = 4. So v₁:v₂ = $\sqrt{3}$:$\sqrt{4}$ = $\sqrt{3}$:2. The order follows the radii — don't swap them.

steps.tip: When v — $\sqrt{s}$omething, apply — to every term in the ratio.

steps.label: DEEPER INTUITION
steps.prompt: Why does the particle on the larger radius need MORE speed to require the same centripetal force?

options.text: Larger radius = gentler curve, so you need more v² to compensate
options.feedback: Exactly. A gentler curve (bigger r) means less acceleration per unit v². To keep F the same, v must increase to compensate.

options.text: Larger radius means more distance, so more speed is needed
options.feedback: Distance traveled isn't the reason. It's about curvature. A larger radius means the path curves less sharply, producing less centripetal acceleration per unit v². So v must increase to compensate and maintain the same force.

options.text: Larger radius increases mass effect, requiring more speed
options.feedback: Mass is identical for both particles — the problem states 'same mass.' The reason is geometric: bigger r = gentler curve = less centripetal acceleration at the same speed. More speed is needed to compensate.

steps.tip: Think of centripetal force as 'how hard the path bends you' — bigger r = softer bend.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: We got v₁:v₂ = $\sqrt{3}$ : 2. The particle with larger radius (r₂=4) has higher speed (2 > $\sqrt{3}$ — 1.73). Does this check out?

options.text: Yes — larger r needs more v to maintain the same centripetal force
options.feedback: Perfect. v² = Fr/m — bigger r means bigger v². The direction of the inequality (2 > 1.73) matches the physics. You're solid.

options.text: No — larger r should need less speed
options.feedback: Larger r doesn't mean less speed — it means more. From v² = Fr/m, bigger r directly requires bigger v² to maintain the same F. The particle with r = 4 is faster (2 > $\sqrt{3}$ — 1.73).

options.text: Can't tell without knowing actual values of F and m
options.feedback: You absolutely can tell. v² = Fr/m gives everything in terms of ratios. You don't need F or m explicitly — they cancel when you divide. The ratio v₁:v₂ = $\sqrt{r}$₁:$\sqrt{r}$₂ = $\sqrt{3}$:2.

steps.tip: After solving, check: does the bigger quantity sit where the physics says it should?

title: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet
subject: mechanics
scenario: Earth has mass 8 times and radius 2 times that of a planet. If the escape velocity from the earth is 11.2 km/s, the escape velocity from the planet will be?

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: You need escape velocity. What's the formula?

options.text: $v_e = \sqrt{2}GM/R$
options.feedback: Locked in. v_e = $\sqrt{2}$GM/R. This form is perfect because the problem gives you M and R ratios directly.

options.text: $v_e = \sqrt{G}M/R — orbital velocity formula$
options.feedback: $\sqrt{G}$M/R is orbital velocity, not escape velocity. You're missing a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. Escape velocity = $\sqrt{2}$ ×orbital velocity. That $\sqrt{2}$ is the difference between orbiting and escaping.

options.text: v_e = $\sqrt{2}$gR only, can't use M and R form
options.feedback: v_e = $\sqrt{2}$gR is equivalent but inconvenient here — g itself depends on M and R, adding an extra step. Since you're given M and R ratios directly, v_e = $\sqrt{2}$GM/R is the cleaner form.

steps.tip: Escape = $\sqrt{2}$ ×orbital. Mixing them up costs you the $\sqrt{2}$ factor.

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they confuse which body is bigger. The problem says Earth's mass is 8× the planet's. So M_E = 8M_P means:

options.text: M_P = M_E/8 — planet is the smaller body
options.feedback: Right. 'Earth has mass 8 times that of the planet' — M_E = 8M_P — M_P = M_E/8. Read ratio statements carefully.

options.text: M_P = 8M_E — planet is the heavier body
options.feedback: You flipped it. 'Earth has mass 8 times the planet' means M_E = 8M_P, so M_P = M_E/8. The planet is the smaller body, not the larger one. Re-read ratio statements carefully.

options.text: M_P = M_E — they're equal since ratio involves both
options.feedback: They're not equal. The problem explicitly says Earth's mass is 8 times the planet's. M_E = 8M_P. The planet is 1/8 of Earth's mass. Read the ratio direction carefully.

steps.tip: Translate ratio sentences into equations immediately. 'A is n times B' — A = nB.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: v_e = $\sqrt{2}$GM/R. Take the ratio v_P/v_E with M_P = M_E/8 and R_P = R_E/2. What is v_P/v_E?

options.text: $\sqrt{M}_P/M_E \times R_E/R_P = \sqrt{1}/8 \times 2 = \sqrt{1}/4 = 1/2$
options.feedback: Perfect ratio work. M goes in the numerator, R in the denominator of v_e² — so when dividing, R flips. v_P/v_E = 1/2.

options.text: $\sqrt{8}$ × 2 = 4 — used Earth values in numerator
options.feedback: You put Earth's values in the numerator instead of the planet's. v_P/v_E = $\sqrt{M}$_P/M_E ×R_E/R_P = $\sqrt{1}$/8 × 2 = $\sqrt{1}$/4 = 1/2. The planet goes in the numerator.

options.text: $\sqrt{1}$/8 × 1/2 = $\sqrt{1}$/16 = 1/4 — flipped the R ratio
options.feedback: You flipped the R ratio. Since R is in the denominator of v_e = $\sqrt{2}$GM/R, when you take the ratio, R_E goes in the numerator: $\sqrt{R}$_E/R_P = $\sqrt{2}$, not $\sqrt{1}$/2. Result: 1/2, not 1/4.

steps.tip: In ratio problems, track what's in the numerator vs denominator BEFORE substituting.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: v_P = v_E/2 = 11.2/2. Final answer?

options.text: 5.6 km/s
options.feedback: Done. 11.2 ÷2 = 5.6 km/s. Clean.

options.text: 2.8 km/s (divided by 4 instead of 2)
options.feedback: You divided by 4 instead of 2. The ratio v_P/v_E = 1/2, not 1/4. So v_P = 11.2/2 = 5.6 km/s. Double-check which ratio you computed.

options.text: 22.4 km/s (multiplied instead of divided)
options.feedback: You multiplied instead of dividing. The planet is smaller and less massive — its escape velocity must be less than Earth's, not more. v_P = 11.2 × (1/2) = 5.6 km/s.

steps.tip: Smaller M and smaller R compete — always compute the ratio, don't guess the direction.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: Planet has 1/8 the mass but 1/2 the radius. v_e — $\sqrt{M}$/R. Is 5.6 km/s (half of Earth's) physically reasonable?

options.text: Yes — less mass dominates, reducing v_e despite smaller radius helping
options.feedback: Exactly. Mass dropped by 8×(pulls v_e down) but radius dropped by only 2×(pushes v_e up). Net effect: v_e drops by $\sqrt{1}$/4 = 1/2. The competing effects partially cancel, giving a moderate reduction.

options.text: No — smaller radius should make escape velocity higher than Earth's
options.feedback: Smaller radius alone would increase v_e (since v_e — 1/$\sqrt{R}$). But the mass drop (8×) overpowers the radius drop (2×). Net: v_e drops by $\sqrt{1}$/8 × 2 = 1/2. Both effects matter.

options.text: No — with such low mass, escape velocity should be near zero
options.feedback: 1/8 the mass is small, but 1/2 the radius partially compensates (smaller R increases v_e). The two effects don't fully cancel — mass wins, giving v_e = half of Earth's. Not near zero.

steps.tip: When M and R both change, check which effect dominates before judging the answer.

title: A particle of mass m and angular momentum L in potential U(r) = kr²
subject: mechanics
scenario: A particle of mass m and angular momentum L moves in space where its potential energy is U(r) = kr² (k>0) and r is the radial coordinate. If the particle moves in a circular orbit, then the radius of the orbit is?

steps.label: WHAT MATTERS HERE
steps.prompt: Circular orbit in a central force with given U(r) and angular momentum L. What two conditions pin down the radius?

options.text: The net radial force = centripetal force, and L = mvr for circular motion
options.feedback: Exactly. For a circular orbit: the force from U(r) provides the centripetal acceleration, and angular momentum L = mvr constrains v and r together.

options.text: Total energy is minimum, and kinetic energy equals potential energy
options.feedback: KE = PE isn't a general rule — it holds for inverse-square forces (virial theorem) but not for U = kr². And minimizing total energy gives the radius but not the full picture. Use force balance + angular momentum.

options.text: Gravitational force balances centrifugal force, and v = $\sqrt{G}$M/r
options.feedback: This isn't gravity — there's no GM/r² force here. U = kr² gives F = -2kr, a completely different force law. The approach is general: any central force = centripetal acceleration, plus L = mvr.

steps.tip: Circular orbit = two equations: force balance + angular momentum constraint.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: How do you get the force from U(r) = kr²?

options.text: F = -dU/dr = -2kr (directed inward)
options.feedback: Right. F = -dU/dr always. The minus sign gives a restoring (inward) force: F = -2kr. The magnitude is 2kr.

options.text: F = U/r = kr (just divide by r)
options.feedback: F = U/r = kr is dimensionally coincidental but physically wrong. Force is the negative gradient of potential: F = -dU/dr. For U = kr²: F = -2kr. The derivative, not division, gives force.

options.text: F = -dU/dt (differentiate w.r.t. time)
options.feedback: dU/dt gives power (rate of energy change), not force. Force comes from the spatial derivative: F = -dU/dr. For U = kr²: F = -2kr.

steps.tip: F = -dU/dr is the bridge from potential energy to force. Burn it in.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: For a circular orbit: |F| = mv²/r gives 2kr = mv²/r. Using L = mvr — v = L/(mr), substitute into the force equation. What do you get?

options.text: 2kr = L²/(m r³), so r⁴ = L²/(2mk)
options.feedback: Nailed it. Substituting v = L/(mr): mv²/r = m(L²/m²r²)/r = L²/(mr³). Set equal to 2kr — r⁴ = L²/(2mk).

options.text: 2kr = L²/(m r), so r² = L²/(2mk)
options.feedback: Track the exponents carefully. v = L/(mr) — v² = L²/(m²r²). Then mv²/r = L²/(mr³), not L²/(mr). You lost an r² in the denominator. Correct: 2kr = L²/(mr³).

options.text: 2kr = L/(m r²), so r³ = L/(2mk)
options.feedback: L = mvr, not L = mv. So v = L/(mr), and v² = L²/(m²r²). Then mv²/r = m ×L²/(m²r²) ×(1/r) = L²/(mr³). The exponent on r is -3, not -2.

steps.tip: When substituting v = L/(mr), write out v² explicitly before simplifying.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: From r⁴ = L²/(2mk), what is r?

options.text: r = (L²/2mk)^(1/4) — take the fourth root
options.feedback: Clean. r⁴ = X — r = $X^{1/4}$. The answer is r = (L²/2mk)^(1/4).

options.text: $r = (L^{2}/2mk)^{1/2} — took square root instead$
options.feedback: r⁴ = X means r = $X^{1/4}$, the fourth root — not $X^{1/2}$. Square root solves r², fourth root solves r⁴. Match the root to the power you're undoing.

options.text: r = (L/2mk)^(1/4) — dropped the square on L
options.feedback: Keep L squared. From v² = L²/(m²r²), the L² carries through the entire derivation. Dropping the square on L changes the answer's dimensions and value. r = (L²/2mk)^(1/4).

steps.tip: Match the root to the power: r⁴ — fourth root, r³ — cube root. Read your own algebra.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: $r = (L^{2}/2mk)^{1/4}$. If you increase k (stiffer potential), what happens to the orbit radius? Does it make sense?f you increase k (stiffer potential), what happens to the orbit radius? Does it make sense?

options.text: r decreases — stronger restoring force pulls the orbit tighter. Checks out.
options.feedback: Perfect. r — $k^{-1/4}$: bigger k means the potential well is steeper, so the particle orbits closer in. The formula and the physics agree.

options.text: r increases — stronger force flings the particle outward
options.feedback: A stiffer potential (larger k) means stronger inward pull at every distance. The particle doesn't get flung out — it gets pulled in tighter. r — $k^{-1/4}$: bigger k — smaller orbit.

options.text: r is unchanged — k shouldn't affect a circular orbit
options.feedback: k absolutely affects the orbit. The force F = 2kr depends directly on k. A stronger spring-like potential confines the particle to a smaller orbit. r — 1/$k^{1/4}$.

steps.tip: Test your answer against limiting cases — crank a parameter up or down and check the physics.

title: A point charge +q at origin; +9q at (d,0,0). Find where E vanishes.
subject: electrodynamics
scenario: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9q is placed at (d,0,0). The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is?

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they assume the null point is at the midpoint (d/2). Why is the null point NOT at the midpoint?

options.text: The charges are unequal — the null point shifts toward the smaller charge
options.feedback: Right. The bigger charge (9q) has a stronger field, so you must move closer to the weaker charge (q) for the fields to balance. Null point shifts toward q.

options.text: The charges are the same sign, so fields never cancel
options.feedback: Same-sign charges CAN have a null point between them — the fields point in opposite directions in that region. But the midpoint only works if the charges are equal. Unequal — shift toward the weaker one.

options.text: The null point is at the midpoint only for opposite charges
options.feedback: Midpoint for opposite charges? No — opposite charges have no null between them (fields point the same way). Like charges can have a null between them, but only at the midpoint if they're equal.

steps.tip: Null point between like charges: always closer to the smaller charge.

steps.label: WHAT MATTERS HERE
steps.prompt: Let the null point be at distance x from +q (at origin). What is its distance from +9q at (d,0,0)?

options.text: (d - x), since the point lies between them on the x-axis
options.feedback: Simple geometry. Point is at x from origin, charge 9q is at d. Distance between them = d - x (with 0 < x < d).

options.text: (x - d), to keep the sign consistent
options.feedback: x - d is negative when x < d (which it must be, since the point is between the charges). Distances must be positive. Use d - x for the distance from the point to the charge at d.

options.text: d + x, since distances add up
options.feedback: d + x would place the point outside both charges, not between them. For a point at position x between the origin and d, the distance to the charge at d is simply d - x.

steps.tip: Always sketch the setup. Label distances from both charges before writing equations.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: At the null point, E from +q = E from +9q. Which equation is correct?

options.feedback: Locked in. The k and q cancel: 1/x² = 9/(d-x)². Cross-multiply: (d-x)² = 9x².

options.text: kq/x = k(9q)/(d-x) — forgot to square the distances
options.feedback: Electric field is E = kQ/r², not kQ/r. The distance is squared in Coulomb's law. Without the square, you get the wrong equation and the wrong null point.

options.text: kq/x² = k(9q)/(d+x)² — wrong distance from 9q
options.feedback: The distance from the null point to 9q is (d - x), not (d + x). The point is between the charges, so x < d, and the distance to 9q is d minus x. Check your geometry.

steps.tip: $E = kQ/r^{2}$. Never forget the square — it's the #1 algebra error in electrostatics.ever forget the square — it's the #1 algebra error in electrostatics.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: (d-x)² = 9x². What's the fastest way to solve this?

options.text: Take square root: d-x = 3x — d = 4x — x = d/4
options.feedback: Beautiful shortcut. $\sqrt{9}$x² = 3x (positive, since x > 0). So d - x = 3x — x = d/4. The null point is at (d/4, 0, 0).

options.text: Expand fully: d²-2dx+x²=9x² and use quadratic formula
options.feedback: Expanding works but is overkill. $\sqrt{9}$x² = 3x (since x > 0). Direct square root is faster: d - x = 3x — x = d/4. Save the quadratic formula for when you actually need it.

options.text: Take square root: d-x = 9x — x = d/10
options.feedback: $\sqrt{9}$ = 3, not 9. You forgot to take the root of the coefficient. $\sqrt{9}$x² = 3x. So d - x = 3x — d = 4x — x = d/4, not d/10.

steps.tip: A² = B² — A = ±B. Pick the sign that keeps x between 0 and d.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: Null point at x = d/4 — that's 1/4 of the way from +q and 3/4 from +9q. Does this make sense?

options.text: Yes — distance ratio is 1:3, so field ratio is 9:1, exactly compensating the charge ratio 1:9
options.feedback: Perfect. Distances 1:3 — fields scale as 1/1² : 1/3² = 9:1. Charges are 1:9. Product: 9q × 1/9 = q × 9/1. Fields match. Elegant.

options.text: No — it should be closer to the bigger charge
options.feedback: The null point should be closer to the smaller charge, not the bigger one. The stronger charge (9q) dominates — you must move away from it (closer to q) for the fields to balance.

options.text: No — the ratio should be 1:9 not 1:3
options.feedback: The ratio is 1:3 in distance, which gives a 1:9 ratio in r², exactly canceling the 1:9 charge ratio. That's why the fields balance. The distance ratio squared must equal the charge ratio.

steps.tip: Verify: (charge ratio) ×(1/distance² ratio) should equal 1 at the null point.

title: A 3% efficient 100W lamp: find E-field amplitude at 5m
subject: electrodynamics
scenario: A lamp emits monochromatic green light uniformly in all directions. The lamp is 3% efficient in converting electrical power to electromagnetic waves and consumes 100 W of power. The amplitude of the electric field associated with the electromagnetic radiation at a distance of 5 m from the lamp will be nearly?

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they use 100 W as the radiated power. The lamp is only 3% efficient. What is the actual EM power output?

options.text: 3 W — only 3% of 100 W becomes light
options.feedback: Exactly. 3% of 100 W = 3 W goes into electromagnetic radiation. The other 97 W is wasted as heat. Miss this and your answer is off by a factor of $\sqrt{1}$00/3.

options.text: 100 W — total power consumed is what radiates
options.feedback: The lamp consumes 100 W total but only 3% becomes light. That's 0.03 × 100 = 3 W of EM radiation. Using 100 W overestimates E₀ by a factor of $\sqrt{1}$00/3 — 5.8.

options.text: 97 W — subtract the 3% loss
options.feedback: 3% is the useful output, not the loss. Efficiency means 3% of input becomes light: 0.03 × 100 = 3 W radiated. The 97 W is wasted as heat. Don't subtract efficiency — multiply by it.

steps.tip: Efficiency problems: always compute useful power output first, before anything else.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: You have radiated power and distance. You need E₀. Which chain of concepts connects them?

options.text: $Power — Intensity via I = P/(4\pir^{2}), then I = ½\epsilon_{0}cE_{0}^{2}$
options.feedback: That's the roadmap. Step 1: spread power over a sphere — intensity. Step 2: relate intensity to E₀ via the EM wave formula.

options.text: Power — Energy via E = Pt, then use E = qV to find field
options.feedback: E = qV is Coulomb electrostatics — it relates to charges, not electromagnetic waves. For EM radiation, you need the intensity-field relationship: Power — Intensity — E₀ via I = ½ε₀cE₀².

options.text: Use E = P/(4πε₀r²) directly like a point charge formula
options.feedback: There's no direct P-to-E formula analogous to Coulomb's law. EM wave fields aren't point-charge fields. You need intensity as the bridge: I = P/(4πr²), then I = ½ε₀cE₀².

steps.tip: For EM wave problems: Power — Intensity — Field amplitude. Always this chain.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: $I = P/(4\pir^{2}) = 3/(4\pi\times 25)$. Now I = ½ε₀cE₀². Solving for E₀ gives:ow I = ½ε₀cE₀². Solving for E₀ gives:

options.text: $E_{0} = \sqrt{2}I / \epsilon_{0c} = \sqrt{2}P / 4\pir^{2\epsilon_{0}c}$
options.feedback: Solid. I = ½ε₀cE₀² — E₀² = 2I/(ε₀c) — E₀ = $\sqrt{2}$I/(ε₀c). The 2 comes from the ½ in the intensity formula.

options.text: E₀ = $\sqrt{I}$ / ε₀c — forgot the factor of 2
options.feedback: You dropped the factor of 2. From I = ½ε₀cE₀², solving for E₀² gives 2I/(ε₀c), not I/(ε₀c). The ½ in the intensity formula becomes a 2 when you invert. This halves your final E₀.

options.text: E₀ = $\sqrt{2}$I × ε₀c — flipped ε₀c to numerator
options.feedback: ε₀c must be in the denominator, not the numerator. From I = ½ε₀cE₀², isolate E₀²: divide both sides by ½ε₀c — E₀² = 2I/(ε₀c). Flipping ε₀c squares your error.

steps.tip: I = ½ε₀cE₀² — the ½ becomes a 2 when you flip. Don't lose it.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: Plug in: I = 3/(4π×25) = 3/(100π). Then E₀ = $\sqrt{2}$ × 3 / (100π × 8.85×10⁻¹² × 3×10⁸). What do you get?

options.text: E₀ — 15.5 V/m (used 100 W instead of 3 W)
options.feedback: You used 100 W instead of 3 W. Only 3% of the power becomes EM radiation. Using the full 100 W gives E₀ — 15.5 V/m — off by $\sqrt{1}$00/3. Always apply efficiency first.

options.text: E₀ — 1.34 V/m (forgot the factor of 2 under the root)
options.feedback: You forgot the factor of 2 under the square root. E₀ = $\sqrt{2}$I/(ε₀c), not $\sqrt{I}$/(ε₀c). That missing 2 from inverting the ½ in the intensity formula cuts your answer by $\sqrt{2}$.

steps.tip: ε₀c — 8.85×10⁻¹² × 3×10⁸ — 2.655×10⁻³. Memorize this product for speed.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: E₀ — 2.68 V/m at 5 m from a 3 W light source. Does this magnitude make physical sense?

options.text: Yes — a few V/m is typical for a weak light source at meters away
options.feedback: Spot on. Everyday light sources produce E-fields of order 1-10 V/m at a few meters. kV/m would be laser territory. 2.68 V/m sits right where it should for a dim lamp.

options.text: No — electric fields from light should be in kV/m range
options.feedback: kV/m fields come from focused lasers or strong transmitters, not dim lamps. A 3 W source spreading over 4π(5²) — 314 m² gives an intensity of ~0.01 W/m², yielding only a few V/m.

options.text: No — 2.68 V/m seems too high for just 3 W of light
options.feedback: 3 W sounds small, but it's concentrated electromagnetic energy. Even the Sun at Earth's distance gives only ~900 V/m. A few V/m for a lamp at 5 m is completely reasonable.

steps.tip: Build physical intuition: ordinary light — 1-10 V/m. Lasers — kV/m or more.

title: E = a(1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t — which frequencies are present?
subject: electrodynamics
scenario: The electric field associated with an electromagnetic radiation is given by E = a(1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t. Which of the following frequencies are present in the field?
goal: Find: ω₂, ω₂+ω₁, ω₂-ω₁ (notably ω₂+ω₁ is present)

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they look at E = a(1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t and say the frequencies are ω₁ and ω₂. Why is that wrong?

options.text: The product cosω₁t · cosω₂t creates new frequencies — you must expand it
options.feedback: Exactly. When two cosines multiply, you don't just read off ω₁ and ω₂. The product-to-sum identity generates sum and difference frequencies. You must expand first.

options.text: ω₁ is not a frequency because it's inside (1 + cosω₁t)
options.feedback: ω₁ inside (1 + cosω₁t) isn't automatically absent — but it's not directly present either. The product cosω₁t · cosω₂t creates sum and difference frequencies. You must expand to see what actually appears.

options.text: Only ω₂ is present since it's the carrier frequency
options.feedback: ω₂ is present (from the '1' term), but it's not the only frequency. The cosω₁t · cosω₂t product generates additional frequencies. Calling ω₂ the only carrier ignores the modulation physics entirely.

steps.tip: Product of cosines = sum of cosines. Always expand before identifying frequencies.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: Which trig identity do you need to decompose cosω₁t · cosω₂t?

options.text: cos A · cos B = ½[cos(A+B) + cos(A-B)]
options.feedback: That's the one. Product-to-sum identity. It converts a product of two cosines into a sum of two cosines with new frequencies.

options.text: cos(A+B) = cos A cos B - sin A sin B
options.feedback: cos(A+B) = cos A cos B - sin A sin B expands a single cosine of a sum. You need the reverse: decomposing a product cos A · cos B into a sum. That's the product-to-sum identity.

options.text: cos²A = ½(1 + cos 2A)
options.feedback: cos²A = ½(1 + cos 2A) is a special case where A = B. Here A = ω₁t and B = ω₂t are different. You need the general product-to-sum: cos A cos B = ½[cos(A+B) + cos(A-B)].

steps.tip: Product — Sum: use product-to-sum identities. Sum — Product: use the reverse.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: Expand E = a(1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t step by step. First distribute, then apply the identity. What do you get?

options.text: a cosω₂t + (a/2)cos(ω₂+ω₁)t + (a/2)cos(ω₂-ω₁)t
options.feedback: Perfect. Distribute: a cosω₂t + a cosω₁t cosω₂t. Apply identity to the product: ½[cos(ω₂+ω₁)t + cos(ω₂-ω₁)t]. Three clean terms, three frequencies.

options.text: a cosω₁t + a cosω₂t + a cos(ω₁ω₂)t
options.feedback: cosω₁t doesn't appear as a standalone term. Distribute first: a cosω₂t + a cosω₁t cosω₂t. The second part becomes ½a[cos(ω₂+ω₁)t + cos(ω₂-ω₁)t]. Three terms, none is pure cosω₁t.

options.text: a cosω₂t + a cos(ω₂+ω₁)t (only sum frequency)
options.feedback: The product-to-sum identity produces both sum AND difference frequencies. cosω₁t · cosω₂t = ½[cos(ω₂+ω₁)t + cos(ω₂-ω₁)t]. You can't drop the difference term — both sidebands always appear together.

steps.tip: Distribute first, then apply trig identities. Never try to shortcut both at once.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: From the expanded form, which frequencies are present in E?

options.text: ω₂, (ω₂+ω₁), and (ω₂-ω₁) — three distinct frequencies
options.feedback: Locked in. The three terms are cosω₂t, cos(ω₂+ω₁)t, cos(ω₂-ω₁)t. So the frequencies are ω₂, ω₂+ω₁, and ω₂-ω₁. Notice: ω₁ alone is NOT present.

options.text: ω₁ and ω₂ only — the original two frequencies
options.feedback: ω₁ alone is NOT present — look at your expansion. There's no standalone cosω₁t term. The product consumed ω₁ and created ω₂±ω₁ instead. Only three frequencies exist: ω₂, ω₂+ω₁, ω₂-ω₁.

options.text: ω₁, ω₂, ω₂+ω₁, and ω₂-ω₁ — four frequencies
options.feedback: Count the terms in your expansion again. There are exactly three: cosω₂t, cos(ω₂+ω₁)t, cos(ω₂-ω₁)t. There is no standalone cosω₁t term — it was consumed by the product identity.

steps.tip: After expanding, count each distinct cos(ωt) term. Only those are your frequencies.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: We found frequencies ω₂, ω₂+ω₁, ω₂-ω₁. This looks like amplitude modulation. Does it check out physically?

options.text: Yes — (1 + cosω₁t) modulates the carrier ω₂, producing sidebands at ω₂ ± ω₁
options.feedback: Textbook AM radio physics. Carrier at ω₂ survives (from the '1' in the bracket), and two sidebands appear at ω₂ ± ω₁. Three frequencies total. You just derived AM from first principles.

options.text: No — modulation should only produce one sideband, not two
options.feedback: Standard AM always produces two sidebands — one above and one below the carrier. Single-sideband (SSB) exists but requires additional filtering. The basic (1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t gives both.

options.text: No — the carrier ω₂ should disappear after modulation
options.feedback: The carrier ω₂ survives because of the '1' in (1 + cosω₁t). That constant '1' multiplied by cosω₂t gives a pure carrier term. Without the '1', you'd get suppressed-carrier modulation.

steps.tip: AM = carrier + two sidebands. If you see (1 + cosω₁t)cosω₂t, think radio.

title: Monoatomic gas U = 3nRT: how far does the piston move?
subject: thermodynamics
scenario: The internal energy of a monoatomic gas is 3nRT. One mole of helium is kept in a cylinder of cross-section area 17 cm² fitted with a light movable frictionless piston. The gas is heated slowly, supplying 126 J of heat. If the temperature rises by 4°C, how far does the piston move? (P_atm = 10⁵ Pa)

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they use Cᵥ = (3/2)R for a monoatomic gas. But the problem says U = 3nRT. What is Cᵥ here?

options.text: Cᵥ = 3R — since U = 3nRT, dU/dT per mole = 3R
options.feedback: Sharp eye. The problem explicitly gives U = 3nRT, overriding the standard (3/2)nRT. So Cᵥ = dU/(ndT) = 3R. Read what's given, not what you expect.

options.text: Cᵥ = (3/2)R — monoatomic gas always has Cᵥ = (3/2)R
options.feedback: The problem explicitly states U = 3nRT, not (3/2)nRT. This overrides the standard monoatomic value. Cᵥ = dU/(ndT) = 3R. Always extract Cᵥ from the given U, not from memory.

options.text: Cᵥ = (5/2)R — use Cₚ since the piston moves
options.feedback: Cₚ is for heat capacity at constant pressure, not for internal energy. The question gives U = 3nRT, so Cᵥ = 3R. Don't confuse Cᵥ (from internal energy) with Cₚ (from enthalpy).

steps.tip: If the problem gives U explicitly, derive Cᵥ from it. Never default to textbook values blindly.

steps.label: WHAT MATTERS HERE
steps.prompt: Light, frictionless piston heated slowly. What type of process is this?

options.text: Isobaric — free piston means pressure stays at atmospheric
options.feedback: Right. A free, frictionless piston always maintains P = P_atm. Gas expands at constant pressure. This means the first law gives W = PΔV directly.

options.text: Isochoric — piston is just sitting there, volume is fixed
options.feedback: The piston is movable — it says so explicitly. A movable piston means volume changes, ruling out isochoric (constant volume). The piston adjusts to keep pressure equal to atmospheric.

options.text: Adiabatic — the cylinder is insulated
options.feedback: Heat is being supplied — 126 J. Adiabatic means Q = 0 by definition. This is the opposite of adiabatic. The free piston maintains atmospheric pressure — isobaric process.

steps.tip: Free piston = constant pressure = isobaric. It's a universal setup cue.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: You need the piston displacement. Which law connects Q, ΔU, and the work done by the gas?

options.text: First law: Q = ΔU + W, then W = PΔV = P·A·Δx
options.feedback: Perfect chain. First law gives W = Q - ΔU. Then W = PAΔx gives displacement. Two steps, done.

options.text: Q = nCₚΔT gives the displacement directly
options.feedback: nCₚΔT gives you the total heat absorbed, which you already know is 126 J. It doesn't give displacement. You need: First Law — Work = Q - ΔU — then W = PAΔx to get displacement.

options.text: W = nRΔT, skip the first law entirely
options.feedback: W = nRΔT is only valid when Cₚ - Cᵥ = R. With the unusual Cᵥ = 3R given here, you'd need to verify Cₚ first. The safest route: W = Q - ΔU, then W = PAΔx.

steps.tip: For piston problems: First Law — Work — W = PAΔx — displacement.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: ΔU = nCᵥΔT = 1 ×3 ×8.3 × 4 = 99.6 J. W = Q - ΔU = 126 - 99.6 = 26.4 J. Now Δx = W/(PA). What must you be careful about?

options.text: Convert A = 17 cm² to m²: A = 17 ×10⁻⁴ m²
options.feedback: Critical step. 1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m², so 17 cm² = 17 ×10⁻⁴ m². Mixing cm² with Pa (which uses m²) is a guaranteed wrong answer.

options.text: Convert P to dyne/cm² to match the area in cm²
options.feedback: Converting to CGS (dyne/cm²) adds unnecessary complexity and error risk. Keep everything in SI: Pascals (N/m²) with area in m². Convert 17 cm² = 17 ×10⁻⁴ m².

options.text: No conversion needed — just divide 26.4 by (10⁵ × 17)
options.feedback: You must convert! P is in Pascals (N/m²), so A must be in m². If you divide 26.4 by (10⁵ × 17), you're treating A as 17 m² — off by a factor of 10⁴. Use 17 × 10⁻⁴ m².

steps.tip: 1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m². It's (10⁻²)² = 10⁻⁴. This trips up everyone.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: Δx = 26.4 / (10⁵ × 17 × 10⁻⁴) = 26.4 / 170 = 0.155 m = 15.5 cm. Does this make physical sense?

options.text: Yes — small cross-section (17 cm²) means gas must expand a lot linearly to do 26.4 J of work
options.feedback: Exactly. W = PAΔx — tiny A means large Δx for the same work. Think of it like a syringe: narrow cylinder, big displacement. 15.5 cm is perfectly reasonable for a 17 cm² cross-section.

options.text: No — 15.5 cm is too large for just 4°C temperature rise
options.feedback: A 4°C rise is modest, but the gas does real work expanding against atmospheric pressure. The small cross-section (17 cm²) amplifies the displacement. W = PAΔx — small A means large Δx.

options.text: No — piston displacement should be in mm, not cm
options.feedback: There's no rule that piston displacement must be in mm. W = PAΔx: with P = 10⁵ Pa, A = 17 ×10⁻⁴ m², and W = 26.4 J, you get Δx = 0.155 m = 15.5 cm. The math checks out.

steps.tip: W = PAΔx — for fixed W and P, small area means large displacement. Think syringe.

title: RMS speed of O₂ at 47°C equals that of H₂ at ___°C
subject: thermodynamics
scenario: The r.m.s. speed of oxygen molecules at 47°C is equal to that of hydrogen molecules kept at ____°C. (M_O₂/M_H₂ = 32/2)

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they plug in 47 directly as the temperature. What must you do first?

options.text: Convert to Kelvin: T = 47 + 273 = 320 K
options.feedback: Non-negotiable. The RMS formula uses absolute temperature (Kelvin). 47°C = 320 K. Using °C gives nonsense — especially dangerous when the answer is negative °C.

options.text: Nothing — °C works fine in the RMS formula
options.feedback: °C does NOT work in gas formulas. v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$RT/M requires absolute temperature. At 0°C (which is really 273 K), the formula still gives a nonzero speed. Celsius breaks at T = 0 or negative values.

options.text: Square the temperature since v_rms involves a square root
options.feedback: You don't square the temperature. v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$RT/M — the square root is over the entire expression, not just T. Convert 47°C to 320 K first, then substitute normally.

steps.tip: Every gas law formula uses Kelvin. No exceptions. Convert first, think later.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: You need to equate RMS speeds of two different gases. What's the RMS speed formula?

options.text: v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$RT/M — depends on T and molar mass M
options.feedback: Locked in. v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$RT/M. Setting two RMS speeds equal means T₁/M₁ = T₂/M₂. That's your entire equation.

options.text: v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$kT — mass doesn't matter for RMS speed
options.feedback: v_rms = $\sqrt{3}$kT/m (with molecular mass m) is valid but dropping the mass entirely is wrong. Without mass, all gases would have the same speed at the same temperature — they don't. Heavier molecules move slower.

options.text: v_rms = $\sqrt{2}$RT/M — that's the most probable speed formula
options.feedback: $\sqrt{2}$RT/M is the most probable speed, not the RMS speed. The coefficients matter: RMS uses $\sqrt{3}$, most probable uses $\sqrt{2}$, mean uses $\sqrt{8}$/π. Mixing them up changes your answer.

steps.tip: RMS = $\sqrt{3}$, most probable = $\sqrt{2}$, average = $\sqrt{8}$/π. Know which — goes where.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: Set v_rms(O₂) = v_rms(H₂): $\sqrt{3}$R×320/32 = $\sqrt{3}$RT_H₂/2. What simplifies?

options.feedback: Clean. Square both sides, cancel 3R: 320/32 = T_H₂/2 — 10 = T_H₂/2 — T_H₂ = 20 K.

options.text: $Only R cancels — 3\times 320/32 = T_H_{2}/2 — T_H_{2} = 60 K$
options.feedback: The 3 cancels along with R. When you square both sides: 3R × 320/32 = 3R × T/2. The 3R appears on both sides and divides out completely. You're left with 320/32 = T/2 — T = 20 K.

options.text: 320/32 = T_H₂/2 but T_H₂ = 160 K (cross-multiplied wrong)
options.feedback: Cross-multiplication error. From 320/32 = T/2: multiply both sides by 2 — T = 2 ×10 = 20 K. Not 160 K. Check: 320/32 = 10, and 10 × 2 = 20.

steps.tip: When equating v_rms of two gases, everything cancels except T/M. Beautiful shortcut.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: T_H₂ = 20 K. But the question asks for the answer in °C. What is it?

options.feedback: Done. 20 K = 20 - 273 = -253°C. That's extremely cold — close to absolute zero. Don't forget to convert back!

options.text: 273 - 20 = +253°C (sign flipped)
options.feedback: You flipped the conversion. T(°C) = T(K) - 273, not 273 - T(K). So 20 K = 20 - 273 = -253°C, not +253°C. The sign matters — this is extremely cold, near absolute zero.

options.text: 20°C (it's already in Celsius)
options.feedback: 20 K is not 20°C! Kelvin and Celsius are offset by 273. T(°C) = T(K) - 273 = 20 - 273 = -253°C. Forgetting to convert back is a common exam blunder.

steps.tip: Solve in Kelvin, answer in whatever the question asks. Always re-read the question.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: H₂ is 16× lighter than O₂ but needs the same RMS speed. -253°C (= 20 K) is near absolute zero. Does this make sense?

options.text: Yes — lighter molecules move faster at any T, so H₂ needs a very low T to slow down to match O₂
options.feedback: Perfect intuition. H₂ at room temperature already zooms at ~1900 m/s vs O₂ at ~500 m/s. To bring H₂ down to O₂'s speed, you need to cool it drastically — all the way to 20 K. The factor checks: T_H₂/T_O₂ = M_H₂/M_O₂ = 2/32 = 1/16.

options.text: No — lighter gas should need higher temperature to match speeds
options.feedback: It's the opposite. Lighter molecules are naturally faster at any temperature (v — 1/$\sqrt{M}$). To match a heavier molecule's slower speed, you cool the lighter one down drastically. H₂ needs very low T.

options.text: No — negative temperature is unphysical
options.feedback: -253°C = 20 K is perfectly physical. Absolute zero is -273°C (0 K). 20 K is cold but achievable — liquid hydrogen boils at 20 K. Negative Celsius doesn't mean negative Kelvin.

steps.tip: Same v_rms — T — M. Lighter gas needs proportionally lower temperature.

title: Volume expansivity β and isothermal compressibility κ: find β/κ
subject: thermodynamics
scenario: At a temperature T, let β and κ denote the volume expansivity and isothermal compressibility of a gas, respectively. Then β/κ is equal to?

steps.label: WHAT MATTERS HERE
steps.prompt: Before doing anything, write down the definitions. What are β and κ?

options.feedback: Solid. β measures fractional volume change with temperature at constant P. κ measures fractional volume change with pressure at constant T. The 1/V and the minus sign in κ are non-negotiable.

options.text: β = (∂V/∂T)_P and κ = (∂V/∂P)_T — without the 1/V
options.feedback: The 1/V factor is essential — it makes β and κ intensive properties (independent of system size). Without it, β would scale with volume, making comparisons between systems meaningless.
You've swapped the variables. β = (1/V)(∂V/∂T)_P varies T at constant P. κ = -(1/V)(∂V/∂P)_T varies P at constant T. Getting the held-constant variable wrong changes everything.

steps.tip: β: how much V expands per degree. κ: how much V compresses per unit pressure.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: Compute β/κ. What happens to the 1/V factors?

options.text: They cancel: β/κ = (∂V/∂T)_P / [-(∂V/∂P)_T] = -(∂V/∂T)_P ×(∂P/∂V)_T
options.feedback: The 1/V in numerator and denominator cancel cleanly. You're left with a ratio of partials, which you flip the denominator to get a product: -(∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T.

options.text: They don't cancel because the partial derivatives are different
options.feedback: The partials are different, yes, but the 1/V factors are just scalar prefactors. In the fraction β/κ, (1/V)/(1/V) = 1 regardless of what follows. They cancel as simple numbers.

options.text: β/κ = (1/V²)(∂V/∂T)_P × (∂V/∂P)_T — the 1/V factors multiply
options.feedback: It's a division, not multiplication. (1/V × A) ÷ (1/V × B) = A/B. The 1/V in numerator and denominator cancel. They don't multiply to give 1/V².

steps.tip: When dividing response coefficients, 1/V always cancels. Look for it.

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: You have -(∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T. Which thermodynamic identity finishes this?

options.text: Cyclic relation: (∂V/∂T)_P ×(∂T/∂P)_V ×(∂P/∂V)_T = -1
options.feedback: The cyclic relation is the power tool here. For P, V, T: the product of three cyclic partials equals -1. This lets you convert the product into a single partial derivative.

options.text: Maxwell relation: (∂V/∂T)_P = (∂S/∂P)_T
options.feedback: The Maxwell relation (∂V/∂T)_P = (∂S/∂P)_T is valid but introduces entropy — unnecessary here. The cyclic relation directly connects P, V, T without needing S.

options.text: Chain rule: (∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T = (∂P/∂T)_P — just cancel ∂V
options.feedback: You can't cancel ∂V like a regular fraction! In (∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T, the held-constant variables differ. These aren't simple fractions — they're partial derivatives. Use the cyclic relation instead.

steps.tip: Three variables, cyclic partials — product = -1. This is your go-to identity.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: From the cyclic relation: (∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T = -1/(∂T/∂P)_V = -(∂P/∂T)_V. So β/κ = -[-(∂P/∂T)_V] = ?

options.text: (∂P/∂T)_V — the two negatives cancel
options.feedback: Clean finish. One minus from the κ definition, one from the cyclic relation — they cancel. β/κ = (∂P/∂T)_V. Pressure change with temperature at constant volume.

options.text: -(∂P/∂T)_V — only one negative from the cyclic relation
options.feedback: Track both negatives. One from κ's definition (the minus sign), one from the cyclic relation (product = -1). Two negatives make a positive: β/κ = +(∂P/∂T)_V, not minus.

options.text: (∂T/∂P)_V — inverted the final derivative
options.feedback: It's (∂P/∂T)_V, not its reciprocal (∂T/∂P)_V. When you rearrange the cyclic relation, (∂V/∂T)_P × (∂P/∂V)_T = -(∂P/∂T)_V, not -(∂T/∂P)_V. Don't flip the derivative.

steps.tip: Count your minus signs like you count your money. Lose one, lose the problem.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: β/κ = (∂P/∂T)_V. For an ideal gas (PV = nRT), does this check out? (∂P/∂T)_V = nR/V = P/T.

options.text: Yes — β = 1/T, κ = 1/P for ideal gas, so β/κ = P/T = nR/V. Matches.
options.feedback: Perfect verification. For ideal gas: β = 1/T, κ = 1/P — β/κ = P/T. And (∂P/∂T)_V = nR/V = P/T. Both paths agree. The general result holds for any gas, not just ideal.

options.text: No — β/κ should equal nR, not P/T
options.feedback: β/κ = nR only holds dimensionally if you ignore that nR/V = P/T. The result (∂P/∂T)_V = nR/V = P/T for ideal gas. It's the same thing — just written differently.

options.text: Can't verify — ideal gas is too simple for this result
options.feedback: The ideal gas is the perfect test case precisely because it's simple. If a thermodynamic identity fails for an ideal gas, it's wrong everywhere. Always verify against the simplest system.

steps.tip: Always test thermodynamic identities against the ideal gas. If it fails there, it's wrong everywhere.

title: Bohr model: ratio of radius of 5th orbit in Li²⁺ and He⁺
subject: quantum_mechanics
scenario: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen-like atoms, the ratio of the radius of the 5th orbit of the electron in Li²⁺ and He⁺ is?

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: What's the Bohr radius formula for a hydrogen-like atom with atomic number Z?

options.text: rₙ = a₀ n²/Z — radius scales as n² and inversely with Z
options.feedback: Locked in. rₙ = a₀n²/Z. Higher orbit (n²) means bigger radius; stronger nuclear charge (Z) pulls it in. This formula is the backbone of all Bohr model problems.

options.text: rₙ = a₀ n/Z — radius is linear in n
options.feedback: rₙ = a₀n²/Z, not a₀n/Z. The radius scales quadratically with n — this is a key Bohr model result from quantizing angular momentum. Linear scaling gives wrong orbital sizes.

options.text: rₙ = a₀ n²Z — radius increases with Z
options.feedback: Z is in the denominator, not the numerator. More protons means a stronger nuclear pull, shrinking the orbit. r — 1/Z. If r increased with Z, heavier atoms would be enormous — they're actually smaller.

steps.tip: Bohr radius: n² stretches it out, Z squeezes it in. r = a₀n²/Z.

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they mix up Z values. Li²⁺ and He⁺ are hydrogen-like ions. What are their atomic numbers?

options.text: Li²⁺: Z = 3, He⁺: Z = 2 — Z is the number of protons, unchanged by ionization
options.feedback: Right. Z = number of protons = atomic number. Ionization removes electrons, not protons. Li always has Z = 3, He always has Z = 2. The superscript tells you the charge, not Z.

options.text: Li²⁺: Z = 1, He⁺: Z = 1 — both have 1 electron so Z = 1
options.feedback: Z is not the number of electrons — it's the number of protons (atomic number). Li²⁺ lost 2 electrons but still has 3 protons. Ionization changes electron count, never Z.

options.text: Li²⁺: Z = 2, He⁺: Z = 1 — Z equals the ionic charge
options.feedback: Z is the atomic number (proton count), not the ionic charge. Li²⁺ has charge +2 but Z = 3. He⁺ has charge +1 but Z = 2. The superscript tells charge, not atomic number.

steps.tip: $Z = protons = fixed$. Ionization changes electrons, never Z.onization changes electrons, never Z.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: Both ions are in the 5th orbit (n = 5). The ratio r(Li²⁺)/r(He⁺) = ?

options.feedback: Clean. Since n is the same for both, n² and a₀ cancel entirely. You're left with Z(He⁺)/Z(Li²⁺) = 2/3. Done in one line.

options.text: (a₀ × 25/3) ÷ (a₀ × 25/2) = 3/2 — flipped the ratio
options.feedback: You flipped the ratio. The question asks r(Li²⁺)/r(He⁺). Since Li²⁺ is in the numerator: (n²/Z_Li) / (n²/Z_He) = Z_He/Z_Li = 2/3, not 3/2.

options.text: (a₀ × 5/3) ÷ (a₀ × 5/2) = 2/3 — forgot n is squared
options.feedback: n² = 25, not n = 5. The formula is r = a₀n²/Z. For n = 5, use n² = 25. But since both ions have the same n, n² cancels anyway — the ratio depends only on Z values.

steps.tip: Same orbit number? Then n² cancels and the ratio is purely Z₂/Z₁.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: The shortcut: when n is the same for both atoms, the radius ratio simplifies to what?

options.text: Just Z₂/Z₁ = Z(He⁺)/Z(Li²⁺) = 2/3 — no calculation needed
options.feedback: Exactly. Same orbit — the problem reduces to one fraction: Z_denominator / Z_numerator. You could solve this in 5 seconds. That's the power of seeing the structure.

options.text: n₁²/n₂² — only n matters since Z cancels
options.feedback: n doesn't cancel — Z does... wait, no. n is the same for both, so n² cancels. But Z is different (3 vs 2), so Z doesn't cancel — it's exactly what determines the ratio. r₁/r₂ = Z₂/Z₁ = 2/3.

options.text: (n/Z)₁ × (n/Z)₂ — multiply instead of divide
options.feedback: Ratios of radii are found by dividing, not multiplying. r₁/r₂ = (n₁²/Z₁)/(n₂²/Z₂). With same n, this simplifies to Z₂/Z₁ = 2/3.

steps.tip: Same n — ratio = Z₂/Z₁. Different n, same Z — ratio = n₁²/n₂². Know both shortcuts.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: We got r(Li²⁺) / r(He⁺) = 2/3. Li²⁺ has more protons than He⁺. Does it make sense that Li²⁺ has the smaller orbit?

options.text: Yes — 3 protons pull the electron tighter than 2 protons at the same orbit
options.feedback: Perfect. More protons = stronger Coulomb pull = smaller orbit. Li²⁺ (Z=3) squeezes the 5th orbit to 2/3 the size of He⁺ (Z=2). The ratio < 1 confirms the higher-Z ion has the tighter orbit.

options.text: No — more protons means a bigger atom and bigger orbit
options.feedback: More protons = stronger Coulomb attraction = electron pulled closer = smaller orbit. 'Bigger atom' in chemistry refers to more electron shells, not individual orbit sizes at the same n.

options.text: No — orbit size depends only on n, not on Z
options.feedback: Orbit size absolutely depends on Z. The formula r = a₀n²/Z has Z in the denominator. Same orbit number (n = 5) with different Z gives different radii. That's the entire point of hydrogen-like atoms.

steps.tip: Higher Z = tighter orbits. If your ratio gives larger r for larger Z, flip it.

title: 3D cubic box: degeneracy of the 5th excited state
subject: quantum_mechanics
scenario: A particle is trapped in a 3D cubic potential well (side a) with U = 0 inside and U = ∞ outside. The degeneracy of the 5th excited state is?

steps.label: PICK YOUR WEAPON
steps.prompt: For a particle in a 3D cubic box, the energy depends on quantum numbers (n₁, n₂, n₃). What's the formula?

options.text: E — (n₁² + n₂² + n₃²), with each nᵢ = 1, 2, 3, ...
options.feedback: Right. E = (π²ℏ²/2ma²)(n₁² + n₂² + n₃²). Energy levels are set by the sum of squares. Different combos giving the same sum = degeneracy.

options.text: $E — (n_{1} + n_{2} + n_{3})^{2}, quantum numbers add then square$
options.feedback: The energies don't add then square: (n₁+n₂+n₃)² ≠ n₁²+n₂²+n₃² in general. The 3D box separates into three independent 1D problems, and energies add: E = Ex + Ey + Ez — n₁² + n₂² + n₃².

options.text: $E — n_{1}^{2} \times n_{2}^{2} \times n_{3}^{2}, quantum numbers multiply$
options.feedback: Energies add, they don't multiply. Each dimension contributes independently: E_total = Ex + Ey + Ez. Since each Eᵢ — nᵢ², the total — n₁² + n₂² + n₃². Products would blow up at high quantum numbers.

steps.tip: Separable potential — energies add. Same sum of squares = same energy = degenerate.

steps.label: SPOT THE TRAP
steps.prompt: Most students get this wrong because they confuse '5th excited state' with '5th energy level'. What's the difference?

options.text: 5th excited = 6th energy level. Ground state is the 0th excited state.
options.feedback: Critical distinction. Ground state = 0th excited state. So the 5th excited state is the 6th distinct energy level. Off-by-one errors are silent killers.

options.text: 5th excited = 5th energy level. They mean the same thing.
options.feedback: Ground state = 1st energy level = 0th excited state. So 5th excited state = 6th energy level. If you count ground state as the 1st excited state, you're off by one — a silent killer in counting problems.

options.text: 5th excited = 5th state counting all degeneracies individually.
options.feedback: Degeneracy is how many quantum states share one energy level, not how you count individual states. The 5th excited state is the 6th distinct energy value. Count levels, not states.

steps.tip: nth excited state = (n+1)th energy level. Always start counting from 0.

steps.label: SET UP THE MATH
steps.prompt: List the energy levels by increasing value of S = n₁² + n₂² + n₃² (each nᵢ ≥ 1). What are the first 6 values of S?

options.feedback: Nailed the enumeration. The tricky ones are S = 11 — (3,1,1) and S = 12 — (2,2,2). Students who skip S = 11 miscount which level is the 5th excited state.

You skipped S = 11, which comes from (3,1,1) and its permutations. Missing one S value shifts all subsequent levels — so what you think is the 5th excited state is actually the 4th. Enumerate carefully.

options.text: S = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 — used n values instead of n²
options.feedback: You used n values directly instead of n². Each nᵢ starts at 1 (not 0), and S = n₁² + n₂² + n₃². So the minimum S = 1+1+1 = 3. Treating S as 1,2,3,... ignores the squared dependence.

steps.tip: Enumerate S systematically: increment one nᵢ at a time. Missing one level ruins the count.

steps.label: FAST-TRACK THE SOLVE
steps.prompt: The 5th excited state has S = 14, from (n₁,n₂,n₃) = permutations of (3,2,1). How many permutations?

options.text: 3! = 6 — all three quantum numbers are distinct
options.feedback: All three numbers (3, 2, 1) are different, so every arrangement is unique. 3! = 3×2×1 = 6 distinct states, all with the same energy. Degeneracy = 6.

options.text: 3 — only 3 permutations like (2,1,1) type states
options.feedback: Three permutations comes from cases like (2,1,1) where two numbers are equal: 3!/2! = 3. But (3,2,1) has ALL different numbers, so every arrangement is distinct: 3! = 3×2×1 = 6.

options.text: 1 — (3,2,1) is the only combination
options.feedback: (3,2,1) is one combination, but each rearrangement — (3,1,2), (2,3,1), (2,1,3), (1,3,2), (1,2,3) — is a different quantum state. All three quantum numbers label independent axes. 6 distinct states.

steps.tip: All distinct — 3! = 6. Two same — 3!/2! = 3. All same — 1. Memorize this pattern.

steps.label: SANITY CHECK
steps.prompt: Degeneracy = 6 for the 5th excited state. Looking at the pattern (1, 3, 3, 3, 1, 6...), does this make sense?

options.text: Yes — (3,2,1) has all distinct nᵢ, giving maximum permutations (3! = 6) for the first time
options.feedback: Exactly. Previous states had repeated quantum numbers — degeneracy capped at 3. (3,2,1) is the first combo with all distinct values, unlocking the full 3! = 6. Higher states can have even larger degeneracies when multiple combos share the same S.

options.text: No — degeneracy can't exceed 3 in a cubic box
options.feedback: There's no cap on degeneracy. Earlier states had repeated quantum numbers (like 2,1,1 — only 3 permutations). (3,2,1) is the first all-distinct combo, unlocking 3! = 6. Higher states can have even more.

options.text: No — 6 is too high; higher states should have lower degeneracy
options.feedback: Degeneracy generally increases with energy, not decreases. More ways exist to partition a larger sum of squares. The pattern 1, 3, 3, 3, 1, 6... will eventually include even higher degeneracies.

steps.tip: Degeneracy grows with energy. The first all-distinct combo always gives 6.
